expanded_description: Emphasizing differences is one of the most frequently tested rhetorical goals on the SAT Rhetorical Synthesis questions. When the prompt instructs students to emphasize how two or more things differ, they must select a synthesis that foregrounds contrasting features, uses language of opposition and distinction, and subordinates or omits similarities that would dilute the sense of contrast. This goal tests the ability to recognize how sentence structure, connector choice, and information selection work together to create a dominant impression of difference. Mastering this goal requires students to understand the structural and linguistic tools that create contrastive emphasis and to distinguish sentences that achieve this effect from those that inadvertently foreground similarities despite including the differing information.

core_concept.principle:
Emphasizing difference is a rhetorical act of foregrounding contrast and backgrounding commonality. The writer designs the sentence so that the contrasting features occupy positions of structural prominence—the main clause, the pivot point of the sentence, the positions of natural emphasis—while any shared features are placed in subordinate clauses, concessive asides, or omitted entirely. The resulting sentence leaves the reader with a dominant impression of difference, even if commonalities exist and are acknowledged.

The structural signature of a difference-emphasizing sentence includes three key features:

(1) Juxtaposition of Contrasting Features: The differing features of the subjects are placed in direct structural confrontation—side by side, in parallel clauses, or in a clear before-and-after pivot. The reader sees the contrast immediately because the sentence architecture places the differences in adjacent, comparable positions.

(2) Contrast-Signaling Connectors: Words and phrases that explicitly signal opposition—'whereas,' 'in contrast,' 'unlike,' 'however,' 'on the other hand,' 'while,' 'differs from,' 'in comparison'—are used prominently. These lexical signals tell the reader that difference is the primary relationship being highlighted.

(3) Subordination or Omission of Similarities: Similarities, if mentioned at all, are grammatically subordinated. They appear in concessive clauses ('although both share...,' 'despite their common...'), in parenthetical expressions, or at the end of the sentence after the contrasts have been firmly established. Often, the most effective difference-emphasizing sentences simply omit the shared features, as they are irrelevant to the rhetorical goal of highlighting what distinguishes the subjects.
core_concept.explanation:
Consider a set of notes about two economic policies. Both were implemented in the same year, both aimed to reduce unemployment, and both were funded at similar levels. But Policy A focused on tax incentives for businesses, while Policy B focused on direct government hiring. A synthesis that emphasizes similarities would foreground the shared year, goal, and funding in the main clause, subordinating the differing mechanisms. A synthesis that emphasizes differences would open with a contrast connector, juxtapose the differing mechanisms in the main clause, and either omit the shared features or acknowledge them briefly in a concessive subordinate clause. The factual content of the two syntheses is essentially the same, but their rhetorical effects are opposite. The SAT tests whether the student can select the synthesis whose structural design consistently points the reader toward contrast.

The incorrect choices for difference goals typically commit predictable errors. Some choices foreground similarities by placing them in the main clause and subordinating the differences. Some choices present the similarities and differences as coordinate, neutralizing the emphasis. Some choices use similarity connectors ('both,' 'share') that actively work against the difference goal. Some choices present the information in a chronological or sequential structure that does not create contrast, even though the information includes differing facts. The correct choice is the one whose structural design—main clause, connectors, sequencing—consistently points the reader toward opposition and distinction.

image.alt: A diagram with two separate circles representing two subjects. The differing features of each are highlighted and placed in direct structural juxtaposition in an example sentence. A shared overlapping area is dimmed and labeled 'Subordinated or Omitted.' Arrows show the structural mapping: contrasting features to prominent positions, similarities to backgrounded positions.
image.caption: Emphasizing differences means mapping contrasting features onto structurally prominent positions and similarities onto subordinate ones, or omitting similarities entirely

sections.section_title: Foundation: The Grammar of Emphasizing Difference
sections.introduction: Before students can evaluate difference-emphasizing syntheses on the SAT, they must understand the grammatical and rhetorical tools that create contrastive emphasis. This section defines the specific constructions, connectors, and sequencing strategies that foreground difference, and provides examples of sentences that succeed and fail at this rhetorical goal.

key_concept.definition:
Emphasizing difference grammatically involves a set of specific constructions that place contrasting information in prominent, juxtaposed positions while backgrounding or omitting similarities. The most effective constructions for this purpose include: the 'whereas' or 'while' contrast construction (placing the subjects in parallel clauses with explicit contrast connectors), the 'unlike' construction (using one subject as a foil to highlight the other's distinctiveness), the direct juxtaposition construction (placing contrasting features in adjacent, parallel structures), and the contrast-first sequencing (opening with the difference and using it as the organizational frame).

Key constructions for emphasizing difference:

'Whereas X, Y' contrast construction: 'Whereas Policy A relies on tax incentives to stimulate business hiring, Policy B creates jobs through direct government employment programs.' The connector 'whereas' immediately establishes the contrast frame, and the parallel clauses juxtapose the differing mechanisms.

'Unlike X, Y' foil construction: 'Unlike Policy A, which offers tax incentives to businesses, Policy B addresses unemployment through direct government hiring.' One subject serves as the reference point against which the other's distinctiveness is highlighted.

Direct juxtaposition: 'Policy A reduces unemployment through tax incentives for businesses; Policy B, in contrast, relies on direct government hiring programs.' The semicolon and 'in contrast' connector create a sharp structural divide between the two approaches.

Contrast-first sequencing: 'Tax incentives versus direct hiring: this is the fundamental distinction between Policy A and Policy B, both of which aim to reduce unemployment but pursue that goal through opposing means.' The difference is named first and frames the entire discussion.
key_concept.context: SAT Rhetorical Synthesis questions with difference goals test whether the student recognizes which of these constructions best serves the goal, given the specific facts in the notes. The student must evaluate the specific candidate sentences offered, not merely identify the presence of contrast language.

categories.description: Detailed analysis of the constructions that best foreground contrast.

examples.explanation:
The 'whereas' construction is the most direct and frequently used structure for emphasizing differences. It sets up two parallel clauses, one for each subject, and the connector explicitly signals that the relationship between them is one of contrast. The parallel structure invites the reader to compare the subjects point by point on the dimensions that differ. The 'whereas' construction naturally subordinates any shared features that might be included in concessive additions.

Example (notes about two theories of language acquisition): 'Whereas the nativist theory holds that children are born with an innate capacity for language, the behaviorist theory argues that language is acquired entirely through environmental input and reinforcement.' The contrast is immediate and structural. The differing core claims are juxtaposed in parallel clauses.

SAT evaluation: When evaluating a 'whereas' candidate, check that the clauses genuinely present contrasting features and that the structure does not inadvertently balance similarities as well. A 'whereas' sentence that presents a similarity in one clause and a difference in the other is muddled in its rhetorical effect.

The 'unlike' construction uses one subject as a foil to highlight what is distinctive about the other. 'Unlike X, Y' sets X as the standard of comparison and then presents Y as departing from that standard. This construction is particularly effective when one subject is more familiar or serves as a natural reference point, and the other's distinctiveness is the main point.

Example: 'Unlike the steam engine, which converts thermal energy into mechanical work through external combustion, the internal combustion engine burns fuel directly within the cylinder, achieving greater efficiency.' The familiar steam engine serves as the foil against which the internal combustion engine's distinct mechanism is explained.

Caution: The 'unlike' construction can sometimes create an unintended implication of superiority or inferiority. 'Unlike X, Y is more efficient' not only contrasts but evaluates. When the goal is simply to emphasize difference without evaluation, a neutral contrast construction may be preferable.

categories.description: The structural patterns that cause a sentence to fail a difference goal.

examples.explanation:
A sentence that opens with shared features, places them in the main clause, and presents differences only in subordinate clauses fails to emphasize difference because the structural architecture is built around commonality. The reader's first and strongest impression is of similarity.

Example: 'Both the steam engine and the internal combustion engine, which convert energy into mechanical work, were pivotal inventions of the Industrial Revolution, though they differ in their methods of combustion.' The main clause asserts commonality (both were pivotal inventions); the difference is mentioned in a concluding concessive clause. The structure emphasizes similarity and fails a difference goal.

A sentence that lists the contrasting features using additive connectors ('and,' 'also') or that presents them as sequential rather than oppositional fails to create the impression of contrast. The information is present but not structurally emphasized as different.

Example: 'The steam engine uses external combustion to convert thermal energy into mechanical work, and the internal combustion engine burns fuel directly within the cylinder.' The 'and' presents the two facts as coordinate pieces of information, not as contrasting alternatives. The reader is not guided to see them as saliently different. This fails a difference goal.

worked_examples.title: Selecting the Best Difference Synthesis
worked_examples.problem:
Read the following prompt and notes, then evaluate the four candidate sentences to determine which best emphasizes the differences. Prompt: A student is writing an article for a history magazine about two ancient legal codes. The student wants to emphasize the differences between the codes. Notes:
- Code of Hammurabi: Created around 1754 BCE in Babylon. Contained 282 laws. Based on the principle of retributive justice ('an eye for an eye'). Punishments varied by social class of the offender and victim. Carved on a stone stele publicly displayed.
- Twelve Tables: Created around 450 BCE in Rome. Contained a set of laws whose exact number is unknown. Based on a combination of customary law and new legislation. Applied to all Roman citizens regardless of social class (in theory). Originally displayed on bronze tablets in the Roman Forum.
- Both codes represent early attempts to codify and publicly display laws.
- Both influenced later legal traditions in their respective regions. Candidate Sentences: A) 'The Code of Hammurabi and the Twelve Tables both represent early attempts to codify laws for public display, the former carved on a stone stele in Babylon and the latter on bronze tablets in Rome.'
B) 'Created around 1754 BCE, the Code of Hammurabi contained 282 laws, and the Twelve Tables of Rome, created around 450 BCE, represented a combination of customary law and new legislation.'
C) 'Whereas the Code of Hammurabi, created around 1754 BCE, was based on retributive justice and varied its punishments by social class, the Twelve Tables of Rome, created three centuries later, aimed to apply uniformly to all Roman citizens regardless of class.'
D) 'Both the Babylonian Code of Hammurabi and the Roman Twelve Tables influenced later legal traditions, the first through its principle of retributive justice and the second through its combination of customary and statutory law.'

step_by_step.explanation: The goal is to emphasize differences. The correct sentence must foreground contrasting features (the notes identify several: retributive justice vs. customary legislation, class-based vs. theoretically universal application, known number of laws vs. unknown number, stone stele vs. bronze tablets, dates 1754 vs. 450 BCE) and subordinate or omit the similarities (early codification, public display, influence on later traditions). The structural signature requires contrast connectors, juxtaposed differing features, and backgrounded commonalities.

Candidate A opens with a similarity ('both represent early attempts to codify laws for public display') in the main clause. The differences (stone stele vs. bronze tablets) are mentioned in a concluding appositive phrase. The structure foregrounds similarity and backgrounds difference. This fails the difference goal. Eliminate.

Candidate B presents the two codes in chronological order with 'and' as the connector. It mentions some differing details (dates, number of laws, basis) but does so in a neutral, coordinate structure that does not create contrast. The relationship is sequential and additive, not oppositional. This does not achieve the goal of emphasizing differences. Eliminate.

Candidate C opens with 'Whereas,' immediately establishing the contrast frame. The sentence juxtaposes the two codes on fundamental differences: the basis of justice (retributive vs. uniform application), the treatment of social class (varied by class vs. applied regardless of class), and the temporal relationship (created three centuries later). The similarities are omitted, which is entirely appropriate for a difference goal. The structure, connectors, and information selection all serve contrast. Keep.

Candidate D opens with 'Both... influenced later legal traditions,' placing a similarity in the main clause. The differences are mentioned in a concluding participial phrase ('the first through... and the second through...'). The structure emphasizes similarity and treats differences as elaborative detail. This fails the difference goal. Eliminate.

worked_examples.final_answer: Candidate C is the best synthesis for emphasizing differences. It uses the 'whereas' contrast construction, juxtaposes the codes on their fundamental differing principles, and omits the shared features that would distract from the contrast. Candidates A and D foreground similarities, failing the goal. Candidate B presents information in a neutral coordinate structure that does not create the required contrastive emphasis. The correct choice is the one whose architecture is consistently organized around opposition and distinction.
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example demonstrates the comparative evaluation of difference syntheses. The correct choice uses the structural signature of contrast: a contrast connector, juxtaposed differing features in prominent positions, and the omission of similarities. Choices that foreground similarity or that present differences without structural contrast fail the goal.

image.alt: A table with two columns: 'Effective Difference Constructions' and 'Ineffective Difference Constructions.' Each row shows an example sentence with the structural features annotated and the rhetorical effect described.
image.caption: Effective difference constructions juxtapose contrasting features with contrast connectors; ineffective ones foreground similarities or present differences without structural contrast

sections.introduction: This section provides practice with SAT Rhetorical Synthesis questions that explicitly require emphasizing differences. Students will work through prompts with varying types of notes and evaluate candidate sentences for their effectiveness in foregrounding contrast.

key_concept.definition: SAT difference-goal questions ask students to select the sentence that best emphasizes how two or more subjects differ. The strategic approach is: (1) Scan the notes and extract the contrasting features—on what dimensions do the subjects differ? (2) Identify the structural signature of difference—contrast connectors, juxtaposed differences, subordinated or omitted similarities. (3) Evaluate each candidate by asking: Does the dominant structural impression of this sentence convey contrast? (4) Select the choice that most effectively and consistently foregrounds opposition.
key_concept.context: Difference goals are the mirror image of similarity goals, and the incorrect choices for one are often structurally appropriate for the other. A student who confuses the two goals—selecting a similarity-emphasizing structure for a difference prompt, or vice versa—will choose the wrong answer even if they correctly understand the notes. Careful reading of the prompt is essential.

categories.description: A prompt where the best synthesis omits the shared features entirely.

examples.explanation:
Prompt: A student is writing a consumer guide comparing two laptop models. The student wants to emphasize the differences between the models to help readers make an informed choice. Notes:
- Model X: Weighs 2.8 pounds. Battery lasts up to 12 hours. Has a 13-inch display. Uses a solid-state drive. Costs $1,200.
- Model Y: Weighs 4.2 pounds. Battery lasts up to 8 hours. Has a 15-inch display. Uses a solid-state drive. Costs $950.
- Both models use the same operating system.
- Both models include a one-year warranty.
- Both models are available in silver or black.

Candidate Sentences (abbreviated for analysis):
A) 'Model X and Model Y, which share the same operating system, one-year warranty, and color options, differ in weight, battery life, display size, and price.'
B) 'Whereas Model X weighs only 2.8 pounds and offers up to 12 hours of battery life in a 13-inch display for $1,200, Model Y is heavier at 4.2 pounds with an 8-hour battery and a larger 15-inch display, but costs $250 less.'
C) 'Both available in silver or black with a one-year warranty, Model X costs $1,200 while Model Y costs $950, and both have solid-state drives.'

The notes contain several differences (weight, battery life, display size, price) and several similarities (solid-state drive, operating system, warranty, color options). The goal is to emphasize differences for consumer comparison. Candidate B is the strongest synthesis: it opens with 'Whereas,' juxtaposes the models on their differing specifications in a detailed parallel structure, and omits the similarities entirely. The reader receives exactly the information needed to compare the models on their differences. Candidate A opens with an extended list of similarities in the main clause and tacks the differences onto the end in a weak predicate. The structural emphasis is on what the models share, not what distinguishes them. Candidate C foregrounds the similarities (color, warranty) and mentions only one difference (price) in the main clause, with another difference (weight, battery, display) omitted entirely. Both A and C fail to achieve the difference goal effectively.

image.alt: An annotated practice question with the prompt's difference goal highlighted, the contrasting features in the notes circled, and candidate sentences analyzed for their structural emphasis on contrast.
image.caption: SAT difference-goal questions require selecting the sentence that foregrounds contrasting features through juxtaposition, contrast connectors, and the omission or subordination of similarities

summary:
Emphasizing differences is a rhetorical goal that requires the writer to design a sentence whose structural architecture foregrounds what distinguishes subjects from each other and backgrounds or omits what they share. The correct synthesis for a difference goal juxtaposes contrasting features in prominent structural positions, uses contrast-signaling connectors such as 'whereas,' 'unlike,' and 'in contrast,' and subordinates or omits similarities so that they do not dilute the dominant impression of opposition. The sentence should leave the reader with a clear sense of how the subjects differ, on what dimensions, and in what direction.

The most effective constructions for emphasizing difference are the 'whereas' juxtaposition (parallel clauses with explicit contrast marking), the 'unlike' foil construction (using one subject as the standard against which the other's distinctiveness is measured), the direct juxtaposition (placing contrasting features adjacent to each other with a contrast connector), and the contrast-first sequencing (opening with the difference to frame the entire discussion). These constructions make opposition the sentence's central organizing principle. Ineffective constructions for difference goals include similarity-first structures that place commonalities in the main clause, neutral coordinate structures that present differences without structural contrast, and chronological or sequential structures that do not create the impression of opposition.

SAT difference-goal questions require the student to evaluate candidate sentences against the structural signature of contrast. The correct choice is the one whose architecture is built around opposition from its opening connector through its juxtaposed clauses to its omission of distracting similarities. When multiple choices include the contrasting information, the decisive factor is structural design: which sentence creates the strongest and most consistent impression of difference. Mastery of this skill is mastery of the rhetorical principle that contrast is created not by the mere presence of differing information, but by the structural and linguistic devices that frame that information as oppositional and guide the reader to see the subjects as fundamentally distinguished from each other.
